evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-009

core.when: When calculating volatility measures using True Range
core.action: Use the complete True Range formula: max(High-Low, |High-PrevClose|, |Low-PrevClose|); do not simplify to High-Low only; for the first bar without previous close, use High-Low as the fallback

consequence.description: Simplified High-Low range misses overnight gaps, causing volatility underestimation; this propagates into ATR, Bollinger Bands, and position sizing calculations, leading to incorrect risk estimates and over-leveraged positions

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-034

core.when: When processing data inputs in the framework
core.action: Assume the framework automatically detects stale data and handles data expiry — the framework does not implement stale data detection or expiry mechanisms

consequence.description: Without stale data detection, the framework may process outdated market data that no longer reflects current market conditions, causing strategies to trade on expired information and produce incorrect signals

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-011

core.when: When implementing data ingestion pipelines
core.action: Implement data freshness validation by checking timestamp fields (e.g., data_date, timestamp) against current time, and reject or flag data older than the configured stale_threshold (e.g., > 5 minutes for intraday data)

consequence.description: Without data freshness validation, stale market data causes strategies to generate signals based on outdated prices, leading to failed trade execution or positions based on expired information

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-011

core.when: When implementing any stochastic operations or random sampling in backtesting
core.action: Assume reproducibility is guaranteed without explicit random seed configuration — the framework does not automatically set random seeds for each random number generators

consequence.description: Without explicit random seed coverage, backtest results become non-reproducible; different runs produce varying equity curves and performance metrics, making it impossible to validate strategy consistency or compare strategy improvements

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-014

core.when: When running backtests or simulations with stochastic components
core.action: Set random seed explicitly via framework seed function before stochastic operations, and verify each RNG sources (numpy.random, random, torch.random, etc.) are seeded consistently

consequence.description: Without full random seed coverage, backtests produce non-reproducible results across runs; the same strategy may show different Sharpe ratios and drawdowns, making it impossible to verify strategy stability or compare parameter changes

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-014

core.when: When loading models or datasets for strategy execution
core.action: Assume the framework automatically binds and tracks model versions to data versions — the framework does not implement version snapshot binding between models and data

consequence.description: Without model-data version binding, running a model trained on older data with newer market conditions may produce unpredictable results, and it becomes impossible to reproduce specific backtest runs when model or data versions change

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-015

core.when: When implementing model versioning and data management
core.action: Implement version snapshot binding by capturing model version hash and data version hash at model load time, and log them alongside backtest results to verify reproducibility

consequence.description: Without version snapshot binding, mixing different model versions with different data versions causes non-reproducible backtests; changing model or data without tracking produces inconsistent results that cannot be validated or audited

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-015

core.when: When implementing PPO (Percentage Price Oscillator) indicator calculations
core.action: Calculate PPO using the formula ((fast_EMA - slow_EMA) / slow_EMA) * 100 to normalize momentum as a percentage of price, enabling cross-asset comparison of momentum signals

consequence.description: Incorrect PPO formula produces wrong momentum normalization; using absolute MACD values instead of percentage causes PPO signals to be incomparable across different price levels and assets, leading to suboptimal asset selection in multi-asset strategies

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-040

core.when: When implementing Ultimate Oscillator calculations
core.action: Use periods 7, 14, and 28 with corresponding weights 4, 2, and 1 to calculate the weighted average of BP/TR ratios across three timeframes

consequence.description: Using incorrect Ultimate Oscillator periods or weights (not 7/14/28 with 4/2/1 weighting) produces false signals that deviate from the standard formula, reducing the oscillator's effectiveness at filtering noise and confirming momentum across multiple timeframes

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-048

core.when: When implementing or modifying Awesome Oscillator calculation in ta/momentum.py
core.action: Use median price (H+L)/2 rather than close price, combined with fixed windows w1=5 and w2=34 (representing one week and one month of daily data)

consequence.description: Using close instead of median price reduces noise reduction effect; using different windows deviates from Bill Williams' original formula and produces non-standard indicator values that may not match mainstream technical analysis libraries

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-052

core.when: When implementing Stochastic RSI calculation in ta/momentum.py
core.action: Apply stochastic oscillator normalization formula to RSI values themselves (not raw prices), producing output normalized to [0,1] range within the rolling window's RSI extremes

consequence.description: Applying stochastic normalization to prices instead of RSI values fundamentally changes the indicator meaning, producing a momentum oscillator instead of an RSI position indicator; strategies expecting RSI extremes analysis will receive incorrect signals

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-054

core.when: When implementing SMA calculation in ta/utils.py
core.action: Use rolling window mean with min_periods parameter to allow partial calculations before window is complete; verify min_periods <= periods and both are positive integers >= 1

consequence.description: Requiring full periods for all SMA calculations would produce excessive NAs at series start, breaking downstream indicators and strategies that depend on SMA values being available earlier

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-057

core.when: When implementing WMA calculation in ta/trend.py
core.action: Apply linear weight formula weight_i = 2*i/(n*(n+1)) where i ranges from 1 to n, ensuring weights sum to 1.0 and are strictly increasing (most recent price gets highest weight)

consequence.description: Using non-linear weights (exponential, square root, or equal weights) changes WMA's sensitivity to recent price changes, altering signal timing and potentially causing strategies to generate trades at different points than expected

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-059

core.when: When implementing volume-based features in feature_aggregation
core.action: Use On-Balance Volume (OBV) cumulative signed volume logic where positive days add to running total and negative days subtract, preserving directional volume information

consequence.description: Using raw volume totals loses directional information; strategies expecting OBV divergence detection (price up but volume down signals weakness) will fail to detect momentum divergence, missing critical trend reversal signals

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-001

core.when: When implementing Bollinger Band feature extraction
core.action: Return each four values: upper_band, middle_band, lower_band, and bandwidth_percentage; the bandwidth_percentage enables volatility regime detection independent of absolute price levels

consequence.description: Omitting bandwidth_percentage breaks volatility regime detection strategies that rely on normalized band width; dropping any band loses either breakout or mean-reversion signal support

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-006

core.when: When middle band equals zero in Bollinger Band calculation
core.action: Handle division by zero gracefully — bandwidth calculation (bandwidth = (upper - lower) / middle) must not raise an exception; return null or skip the record when middle_band is zero

consequence.description: Division by zero crashes the feature extraction when middle band is zero (occurs during certain market conditions or data artifacts); unhandled exception prevents backtest from running

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-006

core.when: When implementing Williams %R indicator
core.action: Apply the negative scaling (multiply by -100) — the indicator MUST output values in the range 0 to -100 where values above -20 indicate overbought and below -80 indicate oversold; do not apply positive scaling (0 to 100)

consequence.description: Removing the negative sign inverts the oscillator scale, making overbought conditions appear near -100 and oversold near 0 — all strategy thresholds become inverted, causing opposite trading signals in live execution

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-019

validation_threshold: output range [0, -100] with -20 overbought boundary, -80 oversold boundary

core.when: When using Williams %R output for trading signals
core.action: Apply the standard Williams %R boundaries: values above -20 signal overbought (potential sell), values below -80 signal oversold (potential buy); these thresholds are specific to the negative-scaled convention

consequence.description: Using positive-scaled thresholds (e.g., above 80 for overbought) on negative-scaled output produces inverted signals, causing the strategy to buy when overbought and sell when oversold

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-019

core.when: When implementing Force Index indicator
core.action: Use default window of 13 for EMA smoothing as specified by Elder — this window provides sufficient smoothing to filter daily noise while remaining responsive to meaningful force changes

consequence.description: Using non-13 window sizes changes the indicator's smoothing characteristics; shorter windows increase sensitivity to daily fluctuations while longer windows follow longer trends, altering the intended signal behavior

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-022

core.when: When implementing Volume Price Trend indicator
core.action: Calculate VPT as cumulative sum of (close_pct_change * volume) with no default smoothing applied — preserve the raw cumulative nature of the indicator

consequence.description: Applying default EMA smoothing to VPT fundamentally changes its interpretation from cumulative money flow to smoothed momentum; the indicator no longer represents true cumulative flow behavior

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-023

core.when: When using VPT output for trading decisions
core.action: Apply custom moving average smoothing externally if needed — the raw unsmoothed VPT shows true cumulative flow; if smoothed version is required, apply user-defined MA after obtaining the base VPT value

consequence.description: Misinterpreting unsmoothed VPT volatility as noise may lead to unnecessary filtering or over-smoothing, obscuring genuine cumulative flow divergences that indicate trend reversals

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-023

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Aroon indicator calculations in backtesting
core.action: Verify that the Aroon window parameter matches the intended strategy timeframe; default window=25 periods assumes one trading month lookback; adjust window to align with specific trend capture objectives (e.g., 14 for shorter-term, 50+ for longer-term trends)

consequence.description: Using default Aroon window=25 with a short-term strategy causes laggy signals and missed opportunities; using a short window with a long-term strategy introduces excessive noise and false signals

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-027

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Vortex Indicator calculations in backtesting
core.action: Verify that the Vortex window parameter matches the intended strategy timeframe; default window=14 periods assumes two trading weeks; adjust window to balance responsiveness vs reliability for specific market conditions

consequence.description: Using default VI window=14 with highly volatile assets causes whipsaw trades and excessive false signals; using a longer window reduces responsiveness, causing delayed entries and exits

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-028

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Keltner Channel calculations in backtesting
core.action: Verify that the Keltner Channel multiplier matches the intended volatility capture; default multiplier=2 assumes 95% price envelope under normal distribution; adjust multiplier based on asset volatility profile (higher volatility assets may need multiplier >2, lower volatility may need <2)

consequence.description: Using default multiplier=2 on high-volatility assets creates bands too tight, generating excessive false breakout signals; using too large a multiplier on low-volatility assets creates bands too wide, missing valid breakout opportunities

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-030

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Ulcer Index calculations in backtesting
core.action: Verify that the Ulcer Index rolling max period matches the intended risk measurement timeframe; default period=14 assumes two trading weeks drawdown measurement; adjust period to align with strategy holding period and risk tolerance

consequence.description: Using default Ulcer period=14 with long-holding strategies causes underestimation of maximum drawdown risk; using longer periods with short-holding strategies causes over-sensitivity to temporary dips and premature risk alerts

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-031

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Schaff Trend Cycle (STC) calculations in backtesting
core.action: Verify that each STC parameters match the intended strategy characteristics; default (window_slow=50, window_fast=23, cycle=10, smooth1=3, smooth2=3) assumes medium-term trend following; adjust parameters based on asset class and trading frequency (shorter cycles for high-frequency, longer for swing trading)

consequence.description: Using default STC parameters mismatched to strategy timeframe causes systematic signal timing errors; short cycle values with long-term strategies create noise, long cycle values with short-term strategies cause lag and missed opportunities

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-032

validation_threshold: stc_window_slow != 50 OR stc_window_fast != 23 OR stc_cycle != 10 OR stc_smooth1 != 3 OR stc_smooth2 != 3 → WARN

core.when: When implementing or modifying the CCI indicator calculation
core.action: Use any denominator other than Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) — CCI requires MAD for proper outlier insensitivity and the constant 0.015 to verify 70-80% of values fall within [-100, 100]

consequence.description: Using standard deviation instead of MAD would increase CCI sensitivity to outliers, causing significant deviation from expected statistical distribution and producing unreliable overbought/oversold signals

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-065

core.when: When configuring the CCI indicator parameters
core.action: Verify window parameter is a positive integer >= 1 and constant parameter is exactly 0.015

consequence.description: CCI constant=0.015 (approximately 1/66.7) is calibrated to produce 70-80% values in [-100, 100]; incorrect constant produces non-standard CCI distribution breaking threshold-based trading strategies

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-065

core.when: When implementing or modifying the ADX indicator calculation
core.action: Use Wilder smoothing (recursive EMA equivalent) for DX averaging — standard EMA would produce different lag characteristics and break consistency with Wilder's original DM/TR methodology

consequence.description: Replacing Wilder smoothing with standard EMA alters the lag profile of ADX values, causing systematic divergence from Welles Wilder's original formula and breaking strategies calibrated to ADX signal levels

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-066

core.when: When configuring the ADX indicator parameters
core.action: Verify window parameter is a positive integer >= 1 (standard value is 14 per Wilder's methodology)

consequence.description: ADX window controls DX averaging; non-standard window values alter smoothing characteristics and produce ADX values incompatible with standard interpretation thresholds (e.g., ADX > 25 for trend strength)

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-066

core.when: When implementing or modifying the Vortex Indicator calculation
core.action: Calculate VI using sum of absolute price movement divided by True Range sum — using close-to-close changes or alternative range measures would alter VI's volatility sensitivity and break the upward/downward movement isolation

consequence.description: Alternative price movement calculations change VI's normalization, causing VI+ and VI- crossover signals to occur at different price levels than expected and breaking mean-reversion strategies using VI thresholds

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-067

core.when: When configuring the Vortex Indicator parameters
core.action: Verify window parameter is a positive integer >= 1; VI+ and VI- are separate indicators that must be compared together (not in isolation)

consequence.description: VI+ and VI- crossover timing depends on window size; using only one component or incorrect window breaks momentum reversal detection that relies on VI signal line crossings

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-067

core.when: When implementing or modifying the PSAR indicator calculation
core.action: Use parabolic stop-and-reverse formula with acceleration factor step=0.02 and max_step=0.20 to achieve gradual acceleration without excessive sensitivity

consequence.description: Different step/max_step values alter stop-tightening speed and reversal frequency; excessive step values cause premature reversals while insufficient values result in stops too loose to be useful

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-068

validation_threshold: step <= 0 OR step > max_step OR max_step <= 0 → FAIL; step != 0.02 OR max_step != 0.20 → WARN

core.when: When configuring PSAR indicator parameters
core.action: Verify step is in range (0, max_step] and max_step is positive; larger max_step values increase reversal sensitivity

consequence.description: PSAR max_step=0.20 caps acceleration factor to control reversal frequency; unbounded or larger max_step causes erratic stop behavior making the indicator impractical for risk management

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-068

core.when: When implementing or modifying the STC indicator calculation
core.action: Apply stochastic normalization to MACD values, then perform double EMA smoothing with cycle=10 — single EMA or different cycle lengths alter smoothness and signal timing

consequence.description: STC combines MACD momentum with oscillator properties using double smoothing; single EMA or incorrect cycle breaks the noise-filtering design causing excessive false signals in volatile markets

evidence_refs.summary: a890410 Derived from BD: BD-069

core.when: When configuring STC indicator parameters
core.action: Verify cycle parameter is a positive integer (standard value is 10); cycle controls the EMA smoothing window affecting both noise filtering and signal lag

consequence.description: STC cycle=10 balances smoothness against responsiveness; non-standard cycle values shift the STC centerline crossover timing breaking strategies calibrated to standard 50-level signals
